There are two key attributes that a problem must have in order for dynamic programming to be applicable: optimal substructure and overlapping sub-problems
Optimal substructure means that the solution to a given optimization problem can be obtained by the combination of optimal solutions to its sub-problems
the shortest path p from a vertex u to a vertex v exhibits optimal substructure
Overlapping sub-problems means that the space of sub-problems must be small
consider the recursive formulation for generating the Fibonacci sequence
Top-down approach : This is the direct fall-out of the recursive formulation of any problem
Bottom-up approach : Once we formulate the solution to a problem recursively as in terms of its sub-problems

Matrix chain multiplication is a well-known example that demonstrates utility of dynamic programming
If matrix A has dimensions m×n and matrix B has dimensions n×q, then matrix C=A×B will have dimensions m×q, and will require m*n*q scalar multiplications
Let us assume that m = 10, n = 100, p = 10 and s = 1000
